name: Oshogatsu (New Year)
tagline: Japanese New Year celebration with traditional foods and decorations

overview.brief: Oshogatsu is Japan's most important holiday, celebrating the New Year with family gatherings, traditional foods, and temple visits.
overview.history: Oshogatsu traditions date back over a thousand years. The celebration lasts from January 1-3, with families gathering, visiting shrines, and enjoying special foods.
overview.significance: It marks new beginnings, gratitude, and family bonds. Traditional decorations like kadomatsu and shimekazari are displayed.

howToCelebrate.title: Decorate with Kadomatsu
howToCelebrate.description: Place kadomatsu (bamboo and pine decorations) at your entrance to welcome the New Year deities.

howToCelebrate.title: Prepare Osechi Ryori
howToCelebrate.description: Prepare or purchase traditional New Year foods (osechi ryori) with symbolic meanings.

howToCelebrate.title: Visit Shrines
howToCelebrate.description: Visit Shinto shrines for hatsumode (first shrine visit of the year) to pray for good fortune.

howToCelebrate.title: Exchange Nengajo
howToCelebrate.description: Send New Year greeting cards (nengajo) to family and friends.

recipes.name: Ozoni (Mochi Soup)
recipes.description: Traditional New Year soup with mochi rice cakes
instructions: Add chicken or vegetables, cook until tender
Add mochi and cook until soft
tips: Mochi should be soft but not melted

recipes.name: Datemaki (Sweet Rolled Omelette)
recipes.description: Sweet rolled omelette, a traditional Osechi dish
instructions: Mix eggs, sugar, mirin, and fish paste
Cook until set, roll while warm
tips: Use a makisu (bamboo mat) if available

decorations.title: Kadomatsu Decoration
decorations.description: Traditional New Year entrance decoration made of bamboo and pine.

decorations.title: Shimekazari (Sacred Rope)
decorations.description: Sacred rope decoration hung above doorways.

name: Hanami
tagline: Cherry blossom viewing festival - celebrating spring's beauty

overview.brief: Hanami is the traditional Japanese custom of enjoying the transient beauty of cherry blossoms (sakura).
overview.history: Hanami dates back over a thousand years. During cherry blossom season, people gather in parks for picnics under the blooming trees.
overview.significance: It celebrates the beauty of nature and the fleeting nature of life. The cherry blossoms symbolize renewal and the impermanent nature of beauty.

howToCelebrate.title: Check Sakura Forecast
howToCelebrate.description: Check the cherry blossom forecast (sakura zensen) to find peak blooming dates in your area.

howToCelebrate.title: Plan Hanami Picnic
howToCelebrate.description: Prepare a picnic with bento boxes, sake, and traditional snacks for viewing under cherry blossoms.

howToCelebrate.title: Visit Cherry Blossom Spots
howToCelebrate.description: Visit popular hanami spots like parks, temples, or riverside areas with blooming sakura.

howToCelebrate.title: Enjoy Evening Viewing
howToCelebrate.description: Experience yozakura (night cherry blossoms) with lantern illuminations.

recipes.name: Sakura Mochi
recipes.description: Sweet pink mochi with cherry leaves, traditional for Hanami
instructions: Mix rice flour, sugar, and water
Shape into balls with anko filling

recipes.name: Hanami Bento
recipes.description: Colorful bento box perfect for cherry blossom viewing

decorations.title: Cherry Blossom Arrangement
decorations.description: Create cherry blossom arrangements for home decoration.

name: Obon
tagline: Festival honoring ancestors with lanterns and Bon Odori dance

overview.brief: Obon is a Buddhist-Confucian custom to honor the spirits of one's ancestors. Families visit graves and perform traditional dances.
overview.history: Obon has been celebrated for over 500 years. It's believed that during this time, ancestral spirits return to visit their families.
overview.significance: The festival honors deceased ancestors and strengthens family bonds. Bon Odori (dance) and lantern floating are key traditions.

howToCelebrate.title: Prepare Altar
howToCelebrate.description: Set up butsudan (Buddhist altar) with offerings of fruits, vegetables, and flowers.

howToCelebrate.title: Visit Family Graves
howToCelebrate.description: Visit family gravesites to clean them and offer prayers and flowers.

howToCelebrate.title: Participate in Bon Odori
howToCelebrate.description: Join Bon Odori dance festivals held in communities during Obon period.

howToCelebrate.title: Float Lanterns
howToCelebrate.description: Float paper lanterns (toro nagashi) on water to guide spirits back to the afterlife.

recipes.name: Ohagi
recipes.description: Sweet rice balls with red bean paste, traditional for Obon
instructions: Pound rice to make sticky rice
tips: Rice should be sticky but not too soft
Can be made with different coatings

decorations.title: Butsudan Altar Setup
decorations.description: Set up Buddhist altar with offerings for ancestors.

decorations.title: Paper Lanterns
decorations.description: Create or display paper lanterns for Obon.

name: Setsubun
tagline: Bean throwing festival to drive away evil spirits

overview.brief: Setsubun marks the day before the beginning of spring. People throw beans to drive away evil spirits and bring good luck.
overview.history: Setsubun traditions date back to the Heian period. The custom of throwing beans (mamemaki) symbolizes driving away evil and welcoming good fortune.
overview.significance: It purifies the home and invites good fortune for the coming year. Family members throw roasted soybeans while saying 'Oni wa soto! Fuku wa uchi!' (Demons out! Fortune in!).

howToCelebrate.title: Prepare Roasted Beans
howToCelebrate.description: Roast soybeans (fukumame) until golden brown and crispy.

howToCelebrate.title: Bean Throwing Ceremony
howToCelebrate.description: Throw roasted beans outside and inside your home while shouting 'Oni wa soto! Fuku wa uchi!'

howToCelebrate.title: Eat Your Age in Beans
howToCelebrate.description: Eat the same number of beans as your age for good health and luck.

howToCelebrate.title: Display Masks
howToCelebrate.description: Display oni (demon) masks to represent the evil being driven away.

recipes.name: Fukumame (Roasted Soybeans)
recipes.description: Roasted soybeans for Setsubun bean throwing
instructions: Roast in oven at 350°F for 15-20 minutes
tips: Can be stored in airtight container

recipes.name: Ehomaki (Lucky Roll Sushi)
recipes.description: Thick sushi roll eaten facing the lucky direction
tips: Must be eaten whole without cutting

decorations.title: Oni Mask Display
decorations.description: Display demon masks to represent evil spirits being driven away.

name: Tanabata
tagline: Star Festival celebrating the meeting of two lovers in the Milky Way

overview.brief: Tanabata is the Star Festival based on a romantic legend about two stars (Vega and Altair) who can only meet once a year.
overview.history: The festival originates from a Chinese legend that came to Japan in the 8th century. People write wishes on colorful paper strips and hang them on bamboo branches.
overview.significance: It celebrates love, wishes, and the beauty of the stars. Children and adults write wishes on tanzaku (paper strips) and hang them on decorated bamboo.

howToCelebrate.title: Write Wishes on Tanzaku
howToCelebrate.description: Write your wishes on colorful paper strips (tanzaku) in different colors representing different wishes.

howToCelebrate.title: Decorate Bamboo Branch
howToCelebrate.description: Hang tanzaku, paper chains, and other decorations on a bamboo branch.

howToCelebrate.title: Display Decorations
howToCelebrate.description: Place decorated bamboo branches at your entrance or in a prominent location.

howToCelebrate.title: Stargazing
howToCelebrate.description: On clear nights, look for the stars Vega and Altair in the Milky Way.

recipes.name: Somen Noodles
recipes.description: Cold noodles eaten during Tanabata, representing the threads of the Milky Way
tips: Can add ice to serving bowl

decorations.title: Tanabata Bamboo Decoration
decorations.description: Create beautiful bamboo decorations with wishes and paper ornaments.
beginnerTips: Place where it can be seen
